forward-looking analysis
Perhaps the most common investor mistake is chasing returns by over-
weighting stocks, sectors, asset classes, or strategies that have been successful
in recent years. In fact
rule_text: Avoid extrapolating past performance over multi-year windows; instead base expected return judgments on a balanced mixture of historical averages, theory, forward-looking indicators, and discretionary views rather than recent trend alone.
entry_conditions: Asset has outperformed its asset class over the past 24-36 months
Forward-looking valuation ratio (P/E, yield spread) is in top quartile of historical range (most expensive/lowest yield)
Financial theory suggests mean reversion probability > 70%
Discretionary macro view is non-confirming (i.e., does not support continuation)
exit_conditions: Asset reverts to median valuation ratio

Equity and illiquidity premia are good return sources but should not
dominate the portfolio, especially at times when their ex ante rewards are
slim. Entry and exit valuations matter.
• Investors can
rule_text: A trader should limit exposure to any single return source and only when its ex ante reward justifies the risk, using diversified tilts across value, carry, and momentum with prudent leverage to enhance returns while avoiding overpriced high-volatility assets.
entry_conditions: At least 3 return sources show positive ex ante expectation
Smallest expected return across sources > 2.5% annualized
Volatility of any single source < 12% annualized
Portfolio concentration in any single source ≤ 20%
exit_conditions: Ex ante reward of any source falls below 2.0%

Bond Risk Premium
• Measured correlations between real activity and inflation are noisier than
asset return correlations. In theory, the growth-to-inflation relation is
positive if demand shocks move
rule_text: A trader should adjust bond risk premium positioning based on the stability of the Phillips curve, which is determined by central bank credibility and inflation expectation anchoring. Supply shocks with weak credibility warrant positive BRP (higher yields/bond shorts); demand shocks with strong credibility warrant neutral or negative BRP (bond longs as recession hedges).
entry_conditions: Central bank credibility index (5Y breakeven inflation volatility or survey dispersion) below 25th percentile
Inflation expectations anchor strength above 75th percentile (low dispersion in surveys)
Real activity-to-inflation correlation > 0.3 (demand shock regime)
exit_conditions: Inflation expectations dispersion widens above median

Alternative Asset Premia
5.4.3. Other Topics
■ Selecting outperforming managers
Even if HFs as a group might not add much value after adjustments for
biases and risks, some managers do add value.
rule_text: Allocate capital to alternative asset managers only when performance persistence can be demonstrated over a sufficiently long track record to distinguish skill from luck, accounting for non-normal return distributions.
entry_conditions: Manager track record length >= 10 years
Performance persistence p-value <= 0.01
Sharpe ratio consistency (rolling 2-year) correlation >= 0.80
Non-normal return adjustment: excess kurtosis quantified and risk-adjusted return recalculated
exit_conditions: Performance persistence breaks below p-value > 0.05 over most recent 3-year window
Manager closes fund or locks redemptions
